title: Champ
subtitle: Irish mashed potato with spring onions and warm milk
excerpt: Champ is the simplest of the Irish potato dishes: floury potatoes mashed with spring onions that have been briefly heated in warm milk, finished with a well of cold butter. The milk carries the flavour of the spring onion into every part of the mash. It is lighter than colcannon, without the bulk of the greens, and pairs with everything from roast lamb to fried eggs.
sourceNotes: Traditional Northern Irish dish, close relative of colcannon. First documented in print in the eighteenth century. Standard of Irish domestic cookery, particularly associated with Northern Ireland. Florence Irwin, Irish Country Recipes (1937): champ recipe.

glossaryTerms.definition: The technique of simmering chopped spring onions in warm milk for several minutes before using the milk for mashing. The milk draws the flavour and some of the colour from the spring onions, distributing the taste evenly through the mash rather than leaving it in distinct pieces.

content.text: The difference between champ and mashed potato with spring onion stirred in is the

: heating the chopped spring onions in the milk before mashing draws their flavour into the liquid and distributes it evenly through every spoonful. Stirring raw spring onion through mash gives patches of raw onion flavour rather than a uniform taste throughout.

items.prepNote: Maris Piper or King Edward, peeled and cut into even chunks
finely sliced, white and green parts
60 g for the mash, 20 g cold for the butter well

content.text: Place the {{potato}} potato chunks in a large saucepan with cold salted water. Bring to the boil and cook for 18 to 20 minutes until completely tender. Drain and return to the pan over a very low heat for 2 minutes to steam-dry.

While the potatoes cook, put {{whole-milk}} of milk and the {{spring-onion}} spring onions in a small saucepan. Heat gently over a low heat for 5 minutes until the milk is warm and fragrant; do not boil. Mash the potatoes with 60 g of {{unsalted-butter}}. Add the warm spring onion milk gradually, mashing after each addition, until the mash is smooth and creamy. Season with {{sea-salt-flakes}} and {{black-pepper}}. Transfer to a warm bowl, make a well in the centre, and place the remaining 20 g cold butter in it.

rows.cause: Not enough milk or potatoes cooled before mashing
rows.fix: Add a little more warm milk, a splash at a time, mashing after each addition. Mash the potatoes while they are still hot for the best result.

rows.cause: Milk not heated with the spring onions long enough
rows.fix: Heat the milk and spring onions for the full 5 minutes, or slightly longer. The milk should smell noticeably of spring onion before it goes into the mash.

content.text: Champ is a Northern Irish dish, a close cousin of colcannon but without the cabbage or kale. It is eaten as a side dish or, traditionally, as a complete meal, eaten straight from the pot with butter melting in the centre. It pairs well with braises, roast meats, and fried or poached eggs. In Northern Ireland it is sometimes eaten with a glass of buttermilk.
